articles.title: No Power, but a Promise
articles.dek: The centurion commanded the ship, the captain read the weather, and the owner held the purse. But Paul — the prisoner — was the only one who knew where they were going.
articles.quote: Your faithfulness in the storm doesn't just carry you through — it becomes a gift to everyone riding the same ship.
articles.body:
Every Filipino knows the dread of a rising storm signal. PAGASA announces Signal No. 3, and the whole barangay shifts into a different mode. Schools close. Offices empty. Everyone scrambles — boarding up windows, filling bañeras with water, buying candles at the sari-sari store before they’re gone. You watch the storm tracker on your phone, that swirling spiral inching toward shore, and the worst part isn’t the wind or the rain. It’s the waiting. That helpless feeling of a force so much bigger than you, with absolutely nothing to do but hold on and pray the roof holds.

That is exactly where we find the ship carrying Paul to Rome in Acts 27.

Paul is a prisoner. Chained, escorted, with zero authority over anything. He has been arrested, beaten, falsely accused, and is now being transported to stand trial before Caesar. He has no command of the vessel, no vote on the route, no say in any decision. He is cargo — cargo that talks. And yet, as they sail past Crete and the weather turns uncertain, Paul urges the centurion Julius to stop. It was already past the Feast of Atonement — a Jewish observance that fell in late October — meaning the Mediterranean was entering its most dangerous sailing months. Ancient sailors knew this. Roman maritime law discouraged open-sea voyages during this season. And Paul, a man who had already survived three prior shipwrecks (2 Corinthians 11:25), felt the danger in his bones.

“Men,” he says, “I can see that our voyage is going to be disastrous and bring great loss to ship and cargo, and to our own lives also” (Acts 27:10).

Nobody listens. The centurion trusts the pilot. The ship’s owner wants to press on to a better harbor. And when a gentle south wind begins to blow, the decision seems vindicated — look, the prisoner was just overthinking it. They sail.

They were not sailing long.

A violent storm — the text calls it a northeaster, in Greek Euroclydon, a name that sounds exactly like the disaster it delivers — sweeps down from the island of Crete and seizes the ship. The crew cannot turn the bow into the wind. They give up steering entirely and let the vessel run wherever the gale takes it. They pass ropes underneath the hull to keep the planks from bursting apart. They lower the sea anchor. The next day, cargo goes overboard. The day after that, they throw the ship’s own tackle into the sea. For days — then weeks — they see neither sun nor stars. No way to navigate. No idea where they are. Luke, who is on board and writing this account, strips it down to one quiet sentence: “We finally gave up all hope of being saved” (Acts 27:20).

These are seasoned sailors. Roman soldiers. Experienced merchants. And every one of them has stopped believing they will make it through the night. No one has eaten in days. The ship is groaning. The storm has been hammering them for two full weeks. Hope has officially left the ship.

And then Paul stands up.

He acknowledges he warned them. But he doesn’t stop there. He says an angel of God stood beside him the previous night — and the angel had a word:

“Do not be afraid, Paul. You must stand trial before Caesar; and God has graciously given you the lives of all who sail with you.” So keep up your courage, men, for I have faith in God that it will happen just as he told me. (Acts 27:24–25)

Here is the thing worth stopping on. Paul had already received this promise before the storm turned catastrophic. The angel spoke the night before Paul addressed the crew — but the destination itself had been settled even earlier: God had already declared that Paul would stand before Caesar in Rome. The storm could rage all it wanted. It could not change the ending. If God said Paul would reach Rome, this ship would not sink. Paul did not enter the storm trying to survive. He entered it already carrying a destination.

Now notice something easy to miss: when Paul shares the angel’s message, the promise is not only for himself. God had “graciously given” Paul the lives of all who sail with you — every person on the ship. Acts 27:37 tells us there were two hundred and seventy-six of them. Sailors who had never heard of Jesus. Roman soldiers whose job was to keep Paul in irons. Passengers and merchants with no stake in Paul’s faith and no knowledge of his God. And yet — the promise given to one man in chains became a covering over every single one of them.

Your faithfulness in the storm doesn’t just carry you through — it becomes a gift to everyone riding the same ship.

This changes how you read your own storms. Most of us, when the wind picks up — when the medical report is frightening, when the business starts failing, when the relationship frays past what you thought you could endure — we begin calculating odds. We stare at the waves and forget the word that was already spoken over us. Paul’s calm was not the calm of a man who didn’t feel the wind. He felt it. He was soaking wet, exhausted, and just as hungry as the rest — he hadn’t eaten in two weeks, same as the crew (Acts 27:21). His calm came from something underneath the storm: a word from God that the storm simply could not reach.

Do you have a word? Not a feeling, not a wish — a word. A promise God has spoken to you through Scripture, through prayer, through a moment so clear you still remember exactly where you were standing when it came. If you do, write it down today. A Post-it on your mirror. A note in your phone. Not because it makes the storm stop, but because it tells you where you are going. And people who know where they are going can stand up in the middle of a northeaster and say to the panicking people around them: Keep up your courage.

The storm did not stop when Paul spoke those words. They still weathered more days at sea. They still had to run the ship aground off the coast of Malta and swim to shore (Acts 27:44). None of it was easy or comfortable.

But all 276 made it. Every single one.

Not because the storm was gentle. Because one man in chains was carrying a word the storm could not touch — and he gave it away to everyone within earshot.

Today, whatever signal is flying over your life, take a few quiet minutes and ask: What has God already spoken over me? Find the word. Hold it. Then look around at who else on the ship needs to hear it. Because the promise was never only for you.

articles.title: The Bronze Shields
articles.dek: When Rehoboam lost the gold, he replaced it with bronze — and kept the ceremony going. It is worth asking when we started doing the same.
articles.quote: Strength is a gift from God; it becomes a danger when it makes us feel we no longer need the giver.
articles.body:
Think about the tita who prayed the rosary every night during the dark years. When her husband lost his job and the family was behind on rent, she was at the prayer group twice a week — hands clasped, voice breaking. God was not a routine for her then. He was oxygen.

Then the business turned around. Her children got into good schools. The house in a subdivision got a second floor. She still went to Mass. The Santo Niño was still on the shelf. But somewhere between the promotion and the new car, the rosary found its way into the drawer. The prayer group slot became grocery time. Sunday Mass fit between lunch and the afternoon nap.

Nothing disappeared entirely. The forms remained. The shells were there. But something had quietly left.

Rehoboam would have recognized her.

When Rehoboam inherited the throne from Solomon, his kingdom was fractured almost immediately. Ten tribes broke away to Jeroboam. But Judah and Benjamin stayed with him, and in those early years he built and fortified. He strengthened fifteen cities (2 Chr 11:5–12). He stored food and weapons. He watched his borders.

Then something quietly remarkable happened. When Jeroboam dismissed the Levites and priests from the north — replacing them with priests of his own making — those Levites made their way south to Rehoboam. And not just the clergy. Anyone in the northern tribes "who set their hearts on seeking the LORD, the God of Israel, followed the Levites to Jerusalem to offer sacrifices to the LORD, the God of their ancestors" (2 Chr 11:16). A king who honored God attracted the people who wanted to honor God too. His kingdom grew stronger because the right people were drawn to it.

For three years, Rehoboam walked in the ways of David and Solomon (2 Chr 11:17). Three years is not very long. But it was long enough for him to feel stable. Established. Strong. And that is where the story turns.

"After Rehoboam's position as king was established and he had become strong, he and all Israel with him abandoned the law of the LORD." (2 Chr 12:1)

Notice the sequence. It was not crisis that broke him. It was comfort.

In the fifth year of his reign, Shishak — the pharaoh of Egypt — came with an army that defies easy description: twelve hundred chariots, sixty thousand horsemen, and soldiers "without number" (2 Chr 12:3). He swept through the fortified cities of Judah like a hand brushing sand off a table. He reached Jerusalem. The prophet Shemaiah came with a word both direct and devastating: "This is what the LORD says, 'You have abandoned me; therefore, I now abandon you to Shishak.'" (2 Chr 12:5)

To their credit, the leaders did not argue. They did not blame the previous administration or look for excuses. "The LORD is just," they said (2 Chr 12:6). That humility mattered. The word came back through Shemaiah: "Since they have humbled themselves, I will not destroy them but will soon give them deliverance. My wrath will not be poured out on Jerusalem through Shishak. They will, however, become subject to him, so that they may learn the difference between serving me and serving the kings of other lands." (2 Chr 12:7–8)

They would not be destroyed. But they would not escape unchanged.

Shishak came, and he took. He took the treasures of the temple and of the royal palace. And then he took the gold shields — the ones Solomon had made, the ones the royal guard carried in solemn procession whenever the king entered the temple. Those shields were gone now. So Rehoboam made new ones.

"So King Rehoboam made bronze shields to replace them and assigned these to the commanders of the guard on duty at the entrance to the royal palace. Whenever the king went to the LORD's temple, the guards bore the shields, and afterward they returned them to the guardroom." — 2 Chronicles 12:10–11

Notice what Rehoboam did not do. He did not close the temple. He did not cancel the ceremony. The guards still marched. The procession still moved. The shields were still carried and still returned to the guardroom afterward.

From a distance, it looked the same.

But it was bronze.

There is something painfully human in that detail. Losing the gold is devastating. But the quiet tragedy is that the ritual continued as if the gold were still there. The forms remained. The structure held. And perhaps, after a while, you stop noticing the difference yourself. You get used to bronze. You forget what gold felt like. The Chronicler records this without drama — bronze shields to replace them — and somehow that plain sentence carries more weight than a long sermon would.

Rehoboam had not lost everything. He had humbled himself, and God had shown real mercy. The city was not destroyed. The lineage held. The kingdom continued. But the gold was gone, and the gold was not coming back.

It is worth asking: what in your life was once gold that you are now maintaining in bronze?

Not asking about catastrophic fall — those are easier to see. This is the slower drift. The kind that happens when life becomes good enough that God becomes less urgent. The prayer that used to come from somewhere deep, that now runs on autopilot. The Sunday worship that used to cost you something, that now just costs you parking. The generosity that used to stretch your faith, that now fits comfortably inside your margin.

The ceremony continues. The forms are there. But you have a feeling — sometimes, in the quiet — that the shields are bronze.

Strength is a gift from God; it becomes a danger when it makes us feel we no longer need the giver. That is the Rehoboam pattern. It is not the pattern of the wicked but of the comfortable. And the comfortable are, perhaps, the harder ones to reach — precisely because nothing feels wrong enough to demand attention.

But here is the grace buried in this dark passage: twice the text notes that Rehoboam humbled himself (2 Chr 12:6, 12:12). God saw it. God responded. The city was spared. There are always consequences when gold has been traded for bronze — but the relationship holds. Humility is still the door back.

You do not need to wait until an army is at the gates. The Spirit of God is not a crisis responder only. He is available on an ordinary Tuesday — when nothing is falling apart, when the job is fine and the family is okay and the bills are mostly paid. That is precisely the moment Rehoboam should have taken.

If you sense that somewhere, in some part of your life, you have been carrying bronze and calling it gold — today is a good day to say so. Not dramatically. Not in sackcloth. Just honestly, to the One who already knows.

"They humbled themselves and said, 'The LORD is just.'" (2 Chr 12:6) That is the shortest prayer in this whole passage, and perhaps the most powerful.

The guards are still at the gate. The shields are still raised. Let them be gold again.

articles.title: Where Mercy Finds You
articles.dek: You didn't create the weight you're carrying. But there is a mercy that doesn't wait for you to be ready before it moves.
articles.quote: May your mercy come quickly to meet us, for we are in desperate need. — Psalm 79:8
articles.body:
There is a conversation that happens in Filipino families — sometimes at the kitchen table, sometimes whispered before a relative's house visit — where a parent pulls a child aside and says: Huwag mong kalimutan, may utang tayo sa kanila. Don't forget, we owe them something. The child doesn't always know the full story. They weren't there when the debt was made, when the wrong was done, when the relationship turned cold. But they are the ones left holding it — the way a piece of furniture is left behind when someone moves out. You didn't choose it. But here it is, in your house now.

This is the emotional world Psalm 79:8 inhabits.

Psalm 79 is one of the rawest prayers in all of scripture. It is a lament written in ruins. The city of Jerusalem — the most sacred place in the ancient Jewish world, the dwelling place of God on earth — has been ransacked. The temple has been burned to the ground. The bodies of faithful Israelites lie unburied in the streets. Foreign soldiers have walked through what was once holy ground, and the silence that follows is the silence of total collapse.

The writer, Asaph, does not respond with a tidy theological argument. He prays the way people pray when everything has fallen apart: honestly, messily, with grief and accusation woven into the petition. “How long, Lord?” he opens in verse 5. “Will you be angry forever? How long will your jealousy burn like fire?” (Psalm 79:5). It is the prayer of a man who has stopped performing composure.

By verse 6, he turns outward: “Pour out your wrath on the nations that do not acknowledge you, on the kingdoms that do not call on your name” (Psalm 79:6). And in verse 7 he names the damage plainly: “for they have devoured Jacob and devastated his homeland” (Psalm 79:7). There is no softening here. The city is ash. And somewhere under the grief is an uncomfortable awareness: the generations before them — the ones who built altars to foreign gods, who ignored the prophets, who walked away from the covenant — played a part in this. The people standing in the rubble are carrying what their ancestors chose.

They didn't create the weight. But it belongs to them now.

And then verse 8 arrives.

“Do not hold against us the sins of past generations; may your mercy come quickly to meet us, for we are in desperate need.” — Psalm 79:8

Notice what Asaph does not do. He doesn't promise to be better. He doesn't explain why the punishment has gone on long enough. He doesn't list his people's recent acts of faithfulness to earn credit back. He simply names two things: the burden they inherited, and the state they are in. We carry what we did not choose. And we are desperate. That is the entire prayer.

But look more carefully at the middle phrase: “may your mercy come quickly to meet us.” In the original Hebrew, this verb — qadam — carries a striking image. It does not picture mercy waiting at the end of a long road you must walk before you arrive. It pictures mercy moving. Mercy stepping out ahead, coming toward you, rushing to intercept you on the path. The psalmist does not say, “We are making our way toward your mercy.” He says, “Let your mercy come to us.”

This is worth sitting with, because it quietly upends the way many of us picture spiritual restoration. We tend to think of it as a journey we take toward God — a process of getting cleaned up, making amends, building a track record of better behavior, until finally we are in good enough shape to approach. We prepare ourselves before we knock. We earn the right to ask.

But the psalmist does not have that option. He and his people are standing in rubble. They have nothing to offer. They can barely stand. So he asks for mercy to walk toward them. And then he adds the most disarming line in the prayer: “for we are in desperate need.” In the Hebrew, the word is dallonu — we are brought very low. We are impoverished. We have nothing. There is no religious performance hidden here, no attempt to look better than they are. Just the raw truth: Lord, we are low. Come quickly.

And apparently, that is enough to pray.

There may be something you are carrying today that you did not choose. A family pattern. A generational weight. A set of circumstances someone else made that you simply walked into. Or maybe it is years of your own choices piled so high you no longer know where the base of the pile is. You have been meaning to pray more seriously — once things settle, once you feel more spiritually ready, once you have fixed a few things first. You have been waiting to approach God until you have something better to bring.

Psalm 79:8 is permission to stop waiting.

The prayer the psalmist prays is not polished. It is not the prayer of a man who has his spiritual life in order. It is the prayer of a man standing in ruins, asking mercy to move toward him — because he has no strength left to move toward it. And the remarkable thing is: this prayer is in the Bible. It is preserved. It is offered to us as a legitimate way to speak to God.

You can pray this today. Not next week. Not after you have improved. Right now, in the low place, with exactly the words you have: Lord, may your mercy come quickly to meet me. I am in desperate need.

Filipino spirituality has always understood something that gets lost in busier, noisier forms of faith: prayer doesn't have to be impressive to be real. The most honest prayers in scripture are the desperate ones. The ones with no pretense. The ones that walk into God's presence not with a résumé but with empty hands, and simply tell the truth.

You didn't create every weight you are carrying. But you don't have to resolve it alone, either. Bring it — all of it, inherited and chosen, ancient and recent — and say simply: Lord, may your mercy come quickly to meet us, for we are in desperate need.

Mercy is already moving. It runs toward the desperate place, not away from it. May it find you there today.

articles.title: The Discipline Proves the Delight
articles.dek: Being corrected by God doesn't mean you've fallen from his favor. Proverbs says it's proof that you still have it.
articles.quote: The discipline doesn't mean you've lost his delight — it means you still have it.
articles.body:
You know how it goes at a family gathering. Someone passes the adobo around and Tita Nena, without even looking up from her plate, says: “Anak, ‘wag kang ganyan. Nakakabastos.” You sit a little straighter. You correct how you hold your fork. And somewhere in your chest, a small voice asks: Is she embarrassed by me? Did I do something wrong?

But here’s something you probably noticed later, maybe in adulthood. The cousins Tita Nena has quietly given up on — the ones who showed up late, talked back, or stopped coming altogether — she doesn’t correct them anymore. She lets them pass. No rebuke. No second chance.

She only corrects the ones she still believes in.

That moment — that ordinary, slightly embarrassing moment around a Filipino dinner table — is the key to one of the most misread passages in the book of Proverbs.

Solomon is writing to his son. Not a king addressing a crowd. A father writing to his child the way fathers do when they know they won’t always be in the room. The entire opening section of Proverbs carries this energy: Listen to me. I’ve walked this road. Let me save you from some of what I learned the hard way.

And right in the middle of this fatherly talk, Solomon writes something that could easily be skimmed over:

“My son, do not despise the Lord’s discipline, and do not resent his rebuke, because the Lord disciplines the one he loves, as a father the son he delights in.” — Proverbs 3:11–12 (NIV)

Two instructions. Two wrong responses. Don’t despise it — don’t write it off as meaningless, as random, as God being unkind. And don’t resent it — don’t build a wall of bitterness, don’t decide that what happened was unfair. Two different postures. Both of them wrong.

Why? Because the discipline doesn’t come from neglect. It comes from love.

But here is the line worth sitting with longer than usual: as a father the son he delights in.

Not the son he tolerates. Not the son he has to deal with. The son he delights in.

That word changes everything about how we read this passage. Because most of us, when life delivers something hard — a season of correction, a consequence we didn’t want, a door that won’t open — assume the worst. We assume God is disappointed. We assume we’ve exhausted his patience. We decide the silence or the struggle means we’ve slipped out of his favor.

But Solomon says: the discipline goes to the son he delights in. The correction is not evidence that delight is gone. It is, somehow, evidence that it’s still there.

Think about that. God is not correcting the people he’s written off. He’s not redirecting the ones he no longer cares about. He corrects the ones he’s still walking with — the ones he’s still building something in. A coach who has given up on a player doesn’t bother with feedback. He just stops calling. The correction only keeps coming when someone still sees potential in you. When someone still thinks you’re worth shaping.

Alex Eala didn’t reach WTA No. 28 — the highest singles ranking any Filipino has ever achieved — by being left alone. She got there because coaches pushed. Because rallies were replayed in slow motion and she was told what she was doing wrong, again and again. Because the people who believed in her most were also the ones most willing to say: That’s not good enough yet. Let’s go again. The discipline proved the delight.

This is where the passage becomes more than wisdom literature. It becomes a window into the character of God himself.

When Jesus was baptized — before a single miracle, before any public ministry at all — a voice came from heaven: “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased.” (Matthew 3:17, NIV). Delight came first. Love was declared before the work began.

And yet it was that same beloved Son who went into the wilderness immediately afterward. Who was tested. Who suffered. Who, in the end, was not spared.

Paul writes in Romans 8:32: “He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?” (NIV)

God’s discipline of his Son was not the withdrawal of love. It was love doing the hardest, most costly thing love can do — moving through suffering toward a rescue the rest of us could not accomplish on our own. And if that is what God’s love looks like at its most costly, then what does it mean when he disciplines us? It means he hasn’t given up. It means the work isn’t finished. It means there’s still something he’s building in you that he believes is worth building.

Then Solomon makes a turn that can feel abrupt until you see the connection: “Blessed are those who find wisdom, those who gain understanding, for she is more profitable than silver and yields better returns than gold.” (Proverbs 3:13–14, NIV)

Wisdom and discipline live in the same house. You don’t find wisdom by avoiding hard seasons. You find it by receiving them rightly — by not despising the correction and not resenting the rebuke, but asking instead: What is being formed in me here? What is God teaching? What do I need to see?

This is not a call to enjoy suffering. Solomon doesn’t say love the discipline. He says don’t despise it and don’t resent it. There’s room for it to be hard. There’s room for it to sting. The question is what you do with it when it does. When the job doesn’t come through. When you followed all the right steps and the outcome still wasn’t what you prayed for. When you did everything the elders asked and still ended up the one taking the blame. In those moments, the question isn’t: Has God forgotten me? The question is: What is he forming?

Tita Nena still corrects you because she still sees something in you worth correcting. She hasn’t written you off. She’s still at the table with you.

And so is God.

The discipline doesn’t mean you’ve lost his delight — it means you still have it.

Receive it gently today. Don’t despise it. Don’t resent it. Ask what it’s teaching. The wisdom on the other side of this season is worth more than silver. And the Father at the head of the table — the one who delights in you, not merely tolerates you — is not stepping away.

He’s still building something in you that he believes is worth the work.
